MPIP · Team Submission Slide 1 of 12
The Problem
Why only 9% of plastic gets recycled — despite decades of effort
Only 9% of all plastic produced globally is recycled. The recycling rate has barely moved in years. The real bottleneck is not collection — it is that the machines that sort plastic keep making mistakes.
⬛

Black plastics = invisible

Black pigment absorbs all NIR light. The sensor gets no signal. ~15% of all plastic is black and mostly ends up in landfill.

🔁

Similar plastics look identical

HDPE, LDPE, and PP are part of the same chemical family. Their light patterns overlap so much that 12,000+ AI combinations still failed.

📋

Output is too simple

Current systems output one word: "HDPE." No quality grade, no contamination warning, no recycling value — just a label.

🏛️  New EU Law — Digital Product Passport (2027)

From 2027, every plastic product sold in the EU needs a digital record: what it is made of, its carbon footprint, and whether it can be recycled. The problem: 91% of plastic at recycling plants has no label, no QR code, no identity at all. No existing system can create this passport for an unknown item. MPIP can.

The Problem Slide 2 of 12
Our Core Idea
One question change — completely different output
❌  Current NIR systems ask:
"What type of plastic is this?"
Output: one word → "HDPE"
Wrong 47% of the time on polyolefins
VS
✅  MPIP asks:
"What are its physical properties?"
Output: density + crystallinity + SCB →
quality grade + contamination flag + EU passport

Density

How tightly packed the molecules are.
HDPE: 0.94–0.97 g/cm³
LDPE: 0.91–0.93 g/cm³
PP: 0.88–0.91 g/cm³
Enough alone to sort the three families correctly.

Crystallinity

How ordered the plastic's structure is.
High crystallinity = better quality, higher recycling value.
Used to assign Grade A / B / C to each item.

SCB (Short Chain Branching)

Side-branches on the molecular chain.
PP: ~300  |  HDPE: ~7  |  LDPE: ~25
Single most powerful signal for PP contamination detection.

→

→

crystallinity, SCB

grade assignment

Our Core Idea Slide 3 of 12
The Data We Used
Real laboratory data from NIST — US National Institute of Standards and Technology

NIST NIR Polyolefin Dataset

Open-access, published on GitHub by NIST researchers. Real laboratory measurements — not simulated data.

WhatDetail
Unique plastic samples39
Spectral scans234 (6 per sample)
Data points per scan4,148 wavelengths
Pure polymer samples19 (HDPE, LDPE, LLDPE, MDPE, ULDPE, PP)
Blend / mixed samples20 (HDPE+PP & LDPE+HDPE mixtures)
Ground-truth labelsDensity, Crystallinity, SCB per sample

Why this dataset matters

It is the only public dataset containing both NIR spectra and measured physical properties for the same polyolefin samples. Without the physical property measurements, we cannot train or verify our regressors.

What the blend samples gave us

11 HDPE+PP mixtures (0% to 100% PP in 10% steps) let us test contamination detection at every level — and find exactly when our model raises the alarm.

Technology Stack

Python · scikit-learn (Random Forest Classifier + Regressor) · PCA (dimensionality reduction: 4,148 → 18 components) · IBM Granite 3 8B Instruct (reasoning + grade text) · IBM watsonx.ai (live API, us-south endpoint) · pandas / numpy / matplotlib

Dataset & Technology Slide 4 of 12
Phase 1 — Understanding the Data
Why NIR sorting is fundamentally hard — visualised
NIR spectra of HDPE (blue) and LDPE (orange) plotted across 4,000–12,000 cm⁻¹.
Both curves have almost exactly the same shape — same peaks, same valleys, just different heights.

What this chart proves

HDPE and LDPE — two completely different plastics with very different recycling values — produce spectral patterns that look nearly identical to a sensor. This is not a data quality problem. It is a fundamental physical property of polyolefins.

What we did

Loaded all 234 NIR files. Averaged the 6 replicates per sample to get one clean representative spectrum each. Visualised the overlap problem.

The insight

You cannot classify your way out of overlapping input data. You need to extract a different kind of information — the physical properties hidden in those overlapping curves.

Phase 1 · Data Exploration Slide 5 of 12
Phase 2 — Proving Our Core Idea
Same data. Same sensor. Different question. +10.5% better.
Model A confusion matrix. Each row = what the plastic actually was. Each column = what the model predicted.
The diagonal = correct predictions. Everything else = mistakes.
PP, LLDPE, MDPE, ULDPE: zero correct predictions — the model never once identified them.
52.6%
Model A accuracy
Current NIR (class-label)
63.2%
Model B accuracy
MPIP (property-based)
CapabilityModel AModel B
PP detection 0% — failed ✓ via SCB
LLDPE / MDPE 0% — failed ✓ via density
Quality grade Not possible A / B / C
Contamination flag Not possible ✓ Available

+10.5 percentage points

On exactly the same sensor data. The only change: we asked the model to predict physical properties instead of a label.

Phase 2 · Model Comparison Slide 6 of 12
Phase 3 — Contamination Detection
Catching the problem current systems are blind to
Top panel: predicted SCB value for each HDPE+PP blend. Red bars = flagged as contaminated (SCB above threshold of 100).
Bottom panel: predicted density for the same blends.
The flag triggers reliably from 20% PP contamination onwards.

What happens in real recycling plants

A "pure HDPE stream" that is secretly 20% PP contaminated causes defects in recycled products. The whole batch gets downgraded or rejected — destroying its value. Current NIR systems give zero warning at any contamination level.

What MPIP detected

PP contamination flagged at 20% mixing ratio through the elevated SCB signal. The contamination alarm stays active all the way from 80% PP down to 20% PP.

Why SCB works for this

PP has ~300 SCB. Pure HDPE has ~7. Any blend of the two produces a SCB reading proportional to the PP fraction — it is a built-in contamination meter that class-label models completely ignore.

Phase 3 · Blend Contamination Slide 7 of 12
Phase 4 — IBM Granite AI Reasoning
39 live API calls to ibm/granite-3-8b-instruct via IBM watsonx.ai

What we did

For each of the 39 plastic samples, we sent the predicted density, crystallinity, and SCB values to IBM Granite and asked it to write a plain-English explanation of the quality grade assignment — grounded in those specific numbers.

All 39 are real AI calls

Every record has "reasoning_source": "ibm/granite-3-8b-instruct via watsonx.ai". No templates. Each explanation references the actual property values of that specific sample.

EXAMPLE · 50% HDPE / 50% PP blend — Granite output:
"The blend exhibits a density of 0.9022 g/cm³, lower than pure HDPE's range of 0.941–0.965, indicating a significant PP presence. The SCB of 289.5 CH3/1000C further supports this. These factors contribute to poor mechanical properties."
Quality Grade: C  DPP Eligible: No  Contamination: True
EXAMPLE · H0008HDPE — Granite output:
"The high density of 0.9520 g/cm³ indicates pure HDPE, while the crystallinity of 0.6300 suggests a well-ordered structure, contributing to premium food-grade quality grade and PCR eligibility."
Quality Grade: A  Confidence: 92%  DPP Eligible: Yes
98%
Top confidence score
H0009PP — clear PP, far from all boundaries
91.8%
Mean confidence
All Grade A samples
48pp
Confidence spread
50%–98% — honest uncertainty
16
Edge-case flags

Phase 4 · IBM Granite AI Slide 8 of 12
Phase 5 — Digital Product Passports & Carbon Savings
From NIR scan to EU-compliant legal passport — automatically

18 EU Digital Product Passports generated

For all 18 Grade A/B pure-polymer samples, we auto-generated full EU-compliant DPPs stored as JSON files. Each passport contains: polymer type, quality grade, recycling pathway, carbon footprint, food-contact eligibility, and carbon saving vs. virgin plastic.

What makes this different from existing DPP systems

Every existing DPP system (Digimarc, Circularise, etc.) requires the plastic to already have a QR code or RFID tag from the factory. MPIP creates the passport from the light scan alone — for the 91% of recycled plastic that arrives with no identity.

Average CO₂e saving vs. virgin plastic
71.8%
Carbon saving in single Fortune 500 batch
8,250 kg CO₂e
Verification method (previously)
Supplier declaration only
Unverifiable
Verification method (with MPIP)

Phase 5 · DPP & Carbon Slide 9 of 12
Phase 5D — The System Improves Itself
Feedback from real quality control failures → better predictions next cycle
Red circles = Generation 1 (trained on pure polymers only).
Blue squares = Generation 2 (retrained after 5 QC failure cases were fed back).
The dashed diagonal = perfect prediction. Blue squares are much closer to it.

How the feedback loop works

1. MPIP sorts plastic → logs every prediction
2. Downstream QC finds a failure (e.g. "HDPE" batch contained PP)
3. That failure is mapped back to the original spectrum
4. Model is retrained with the correct label added
5. Next generation is more accurate

46.3%
Density prediction
error reduction
25.0%
SCB prediction
error reduction

After just 1 cycle with 5 feedback cases

A system running for a year, with hundreds of real QC events fed back, keeps getting better. It learns what real-world contaminated and worn plastic looks like — not just clean lab samples.

Phase 5D · Self-Improving Loop Slide 10 of 12
The Black Plastic Challenge — Honest Gap Analysis
What we proved, and what remains to be solved
Panel A: As carbon black content increases, Standard NIR accuracy (red) crashes below 70% almost immediately. MPIP property-based (blue) degrades more slowly. FTIR (green) stays near 95%.
Panel B: At 2% carbon black (typical black plastic): Standard NIR = 64%, MPIP = 76%, FTIR = 95%.
Both NIR values are within the range reported in Becker et al. (2025), Spectrochimica Acta Part B.

Current NIR at 2% CB

64%

−31% from clean baseline. Falls below 70% reliability threshold immediately.

MPIP at 2% CB

76%

−19% from clean. Property-based approach partially survives because density bands are more attenuation-stable.

MPIP v2 Roadmap

Add Raman spectroscopy (insensitive to carbon black) as a complementary sensor. The same IBM Granite reasoning layer and DPP pipeline can absorb Raman features without any architectural change.
